subject: Property Law
lesson_title: Civil Partnership and Cohabitants: Property Rights

questions.question: Which Act created the primary statutory framework governing the property rights of civil partners and cohabitants in Ireland?

options.B: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010
options.D: The Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009

questions.explanation: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 created two separate regimes: a comprehensive scheme for civil partners analogous to the family home regime under the 1976 Act, and a discretionary redress scheme for qualified cohabitants under Part 15. The 1976 Act applies to married couples, not civil partners or cohabitants.

questions.question: Under which Part of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 is the shared home protection for civil partners contained?
questions.explanation: Part 4 of the 2010 Act creates a regime for the shared home of civil partners that is broadly analogous to the family home regime under the Family Home Protection Act 1976. Part 15 deals with the separate redress scheme for qualified cohabitants.

questions.question: What is the consequence of a conveyance of a civil partner's interest in the shared home made without the prior written consent of the other civil partner under the 2010 Act?

options.A: The conveyance is voidable at the option of the non-consenting civil partner
options.C: The conveyance is valid but the non-consenting civil partner may claim damages
options.D: The conveyance is valid provided the purchaser had no notice of the civil partnership

questions.explanation: Under Part 4 of the 2010 Act, a conveyance of an interest in the shared home made without the prior written consent of the other civil partner is void. This mirrors the position under section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 for married couples. The same principles apply: consent must be informed and freely given.

questions.question: Following the enactment of the Marriage Equality Act 2015, which regime governs same-sex couples who subsequently marry?

options.A: Part 4 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010
options.B: Part 15 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010

questions.explanation: Following the Marriage Equality Act 2015, same-sex couples may now marry. If they do so, they are governed by the Family Home Protection Act 1976 regime applicable to married couples. The civil partnership regime under the 2010 Act continues to apply to those who registered civil partnerships before 2015 and have not converted their civil partnership to a marriage.

questions.question: Under Part 15 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010, what is the general qualifying period of cohabitation required before a person can apply as a 'qualified cohabitant'?

options.B: Three years, or one year if there is a child of the relationship
options.C: Five years, or two years if there is a child of the relationship

questions.explanation: Part 15 of the 2010 Act defines a qualified cohabitant as a person who has lived with another in an intimate and committed relationship, not as a spouse or civil partner, for at least five years, or at least two years if they have a child together. These thresholds are frequently tested in the FE-1 examination.

questions.question: Which of the following orders can a qualified cohabitant apply for under Part 15 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010?

options.A: A property adjustment order, a compensatory maintenance order, or provision from the estate of a deceased cohabitant
options.B: An automatic half-share of any jointly occupied property
options.C: A declaration that the shared home is void as against them
options.D: A lien over the property to secure their contribution to the mortgage

questions.explanation: Under Part 15 of the 2010 Act, a qualified cohabitant may apply for a property adjustment order, a compensatory maintenance order, or provision from the estate of a deceased cohabitant. The court exercises a genuinely discretionary jurisdiction and does not apply automatic entitlements. It must consider factors including financial dependence, duration of the relationship, and contributions made.

questions.question: Which section of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 sets out the factors the court must consider when determining a cohabitant redress application, as confirmed in MN v ST [2022]?
questions.explanation: Section 173 of the 2010 Act lists the factors the court must consider when exercising its discretion on a cohabitant redress application, including financial circumstances, contributions, and the effect of the relationship on earning capacity. In MN v ST [2022], the Supreme Court confirmed that these factors are not exhaustive but indicative, and that the jurisdiction is genuinely discretionary.

questions.question: In MN v ST [2022], what did the Supreme Court confirm about the nature of the court's jurisdiction under Part 15 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010?

options.A: The court must award the cohabitant a minimum of one quarter of the shared property
options.B: The jurisdiction is genuinely discretionary and the listed factors are not exhaustive
options.C: The court has no jurisdiction to award a property adjustment order unless the cohabitant is on the title deeds
options.D: The cohabitant automatically acquires a beneficial interest after two years of cohabitation

questions.explanation: In MN v ST [2022], the Supreme Court confirmed that the court's jurisdiction under Part 15 is genuinely discretionary and that the factors listed in section 173 — financial circumstances, contributions, the effect of the relationship on earning capacity — are not exhaustive but indicative. This case is important authority for the nature of the cohabitant scheme and is regularly cited on FE-1 papers.

questions.question: Which equitable doctrines remained relevant to cohabitants' property disputes before the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 came into force, and continue to be relevant where the statutory thresholds are not met?
questions.explanation: Before the 2010 Act and where its thresholds are not met, the law of resulting and constructive trusts governs cohabitants' property disputes. The leading pre-2010 Irish case O'D v O'D applied these equitable doctrines to resolve property disputes between cohabitants. Trust principles remain relevant where parties have separated before the Act's qualifying periods are satisfied.

questions.question: Does the shared home protection under Part 4 of the Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 operate for cohabitants in the same automatic voidness mechanism as it does for civil partners?

options.A: Yes, a conveyance without a cohabitant's consent is void in the same way as for civil partners
options.B: No, the cohabitant's remedy is a court application for redress under Part 15; there is no automatic voidness mechanism
options.C: Yes, but only if the cohabitant has been on the title deeds for at least two years
options.D: No, cohabitants have no protection whatsoever under the 2010 Act

questions.explanation: The 2010 Act does not give cohabitants the same automatic voidness mechanism as civil partners. Under Part 4, a conveyance of the civil partner's shared home without consent is void. For cohabitants, Part 15 provides a discretionary redress scheme requiring a court application. There is no real-time consent requirement equivalent to section 3 of the 1976 Act for cohabitants.
